text: The surgeon makes a small incision approximately four to five centimeters above the umbilicus and below the xiphisternum to assess operability. The peritoneal surfaces, omentum, liver surface, and mesenteries of the small and large bowel are visually inspected for deposits, confirming the absence of visible metastases.

The surgeon extends the incision up to the xiphisternum. The transverse colon is held in the surgeon's left hand, while the assistant holds the omentum and stomach. The surgeon carefully separates the transverse colon and its mesentery from the stomach and omentum, maintaining a distance of five millimeters above the large bowel to prevent heat transmission, continuing the separation near the splenic flexure.

also near the hepatic flexure so that the stomach is totally separated from its attachment to the colon we all know that CS stomach is one of the deadliest cancers that is the second leading cause of death if you see world over incidence it is second commonest in males and third commonest in females and in India it is fifth commonest malignancy in males and seventh commonest malignancy in females prognosis wise the prognosis as compared to breast thyroid prostate stomach cancer prognosis are quite bad the prognosis

The surgeon is near the distal end of the stomach, attempting to separate it from the colon. The procedure involves caucarization to separate the colon and duodenum. The gallbladder is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates visible structures in the surgical field, focusing on the distal stomach area during the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the right gastroepiploic vessels, including the artery and vein. The growth is observed in close contact with the capsule, prompting the removal of part of the pancreas and subpyloric nodes. The duodenum is separated, and an adequate margin of more than one centimeter is ensured before cutting it with a DST stapler, effectively separating the growth from the stomach and duodenum. The surgeon palpates to confirm adequate margins.

I have adequate margin there now you might have heard about duodenal blowout if we need to avoid it then we need to take care of this duodenal stump nicely and touchwood if with more than three hundred CS stomachs there was one duodenal leak which I faced that also the patient had a poor general condition and also patient had presented to us with perforation.

The surgeon staples the duodenal stump and places a protective layer over it, incorporating the pancreatic capsule and part of the pancreas. The falciform ligament is freed and positioned over the duodenal stump for additional protection.

The surgeon places a protective flap over the duodenal stump using the falciform ligament to localize and promote healing. The dissection continues towards the proximal end, ensuring a minimum margin of four centimeters from the tumor. The visual field shows no growth nearby, as the surgeon plans for a subtotal to near-total gastrectomy.

The surgeon uses junction violet to mark the area along the lesser curvature, ensuring clear visibility for incision. After palpating to confirm adequate margins, the anesthetist withdraws the NG tube to prevent interference with the staple line. The surgeon prepares to ligate the left gastric artery and coronary vein.

The surgeon performs a D2 lymph node dissection, removing nodes along the hepatic artery, splenic artery, left gastric artery, and celiac area. The left gastric artery and coronary vein are ligated. The surgeon prepares to cut the stomach using a green cartridge stapler.
The surgeon delineates the area along the greater curvature near the gastro-splenic ligament, preparing to cut the stomach while preserving the omentum and short gastric vessels.
The surgeon uses a linear cutter with a green DST cartridge along the lesser curvature of the stomach. Two intestinal clamps are applied, and a knife or scissors is used to cut and leave an opening.

The surgeon prepares to anastomose the distal part of the stomach to the jejunum along the greater curvature. The excised specimen, including the stomach, omentum, and DII lymph nodes, is visible. Precautions are taken to avoid spillage by suctioning, and the stapled end is reinforced using trio silk sutures.
The surgeon reinforces the staple line by suturing the seromuscular layers of the anterior and posterior walls of the stomach, ensuring additional support to prevent potential leaks.

The surgeon holds the jejunal segment in the left hand and staples it approximately fifteen centimeters from the duodenojejunal flexure. The proximal end is positioned to the right and the distal end to the left, using an NTLC stapler for the reconstruction.

The surgeon reinforces the proximal and distal ends of the jejunal loop with threosilk sutures. The distal jejunal loop is then anastomosed to the remnant stomach, creating a gastrojejunostomy to maintain continuity for food passage from the stomach into the jejunum.

The surgeon holds the colon and creates an opening in the mesentery to the right of the middle colic vessels. Through this opening, the distal loop is taken for gastrointestinal anastomosis. A marked area in blue, approximately three to four centimeters, indicates where the jejunum will be anastomosed to the stomach, and the gastro-jejunal anastomosis begins.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing using 3-0 silk to join the seromuscular layer of the posterior wall of the stomach with the anterior wall of the jejunum, approximately one to two centimeters from the cut end of the stomach.

The surgeon completes the fourth layer of suturing and begins the third layer by opening the lumen of the jejunum, matching the size of the stomach. Using Vicryl-Trio sutures, the surgeon performs an anchoring knot by stitching from the jejunal serosa to mucosa and then from the stomach serosa to mucosa, initiating the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a continuous suturing technique, taking full thickness bites from the serosa to the mucosa of both the stomach and jejunum. The sutures progress along the third layer of the anastomosis, ensuring secure attachment between the two structures.
The surgeon completes the third layer of sutures and begins the second layer, preparing to use conal sutures. The sutures are being tied to secure the anastomosis between the stomach and jejunum.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of conal sutures by inserting the needle from the serosa to the mucosa and then back from the mucosa to the serosa on the same side. A Ryles tube is positioned to guide the placement of the second layer of sutures, ensuring the mucosa and serosa do not protrude. The sutures are left loose initially to avoid incorporating the posterior wall and ensure adequate thickness of the serosa and mucosa.

there are certain indications where you do a palliative surgery when the patient is bleeding or there is obstruction sometimes when the disease is a little advanced what you do is you give new adjuvant chemotherapy and then go for surgery most of the patients their presentation is in the stage two and above. Such patients will require adjuvant treatment in the post-op period with chemotherapy.

I have nearly reached the end of the second layer. Commonest histopathology is adenocarcinoma in the stomach. You may get sometimes squamous cell carcinoma also in the proximal stomach. The disease is most of the time in the body. around forty percent of the time and the distal around thirty seven percent of the time site when it involves proximal stomach we normally do a total gastrectomy when it involves the distal we do a distal radical or a subtotal or a near total based on the requirement here in this case we have done somewhere between subtotal and near total once we have completed we tighten those loose switches which we had placed here

The surgeon begins the fourth layer of suturing, focusing on the seromuscular layer of the jejunum and stomach to reinforce the second layer. The visual process involves continuous suturing of these layers, with an option for stapled anastomosis, where staples are applied and two holes are created for digital anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 silk suture to anchor the edges of the fourth layer, ensuring tension is avoided. The gastrojejunostomy anastomosis is completed, and additional sutures are placed to stabilize the jejunal loop through the rent.

The surgeon performs a jejuno-jejunal anastomosis, located fifty centimeters from the gastrojejunal anastomosis. The proximal jejunal loop is connected using three-zero silk sutures to create a small passage for bile and pancreatic enzymes, ensuring continuity of the digestive tract.
The surgeon prepares for staple anastomosis by creating two holes at the same level in both limbs of the jejunum. The holes are sized to allow the insertion of the stapling device's limbs.
The surgeon uses a cutting cautery to make an incision, then creates a hole with an artery forceps, allowing the surgical limb to enter through the opening.

The surgeon uses the NTLC stapler to perform the jejunal-jejunal anastomosis, ensuring hemostasis with minimal bleeding. After completing the anastomosis, the surgeon closes the small incision made in the jejunum using 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon performs conal sutures by inserting the needle from the serosa to the mucosa on one side, then returning from the mucosa to the serosa. The same technique is applied on the opposite side to secure the tissue layers.
How can we prevent the decrease in incidence of CH stomach? All the dietary habits need to be modified. Avoid infection, batch pylori. If positive, take the treatment.

for it maintain good sanitation good hygiene of food avoid spicy pickled foods stored foods proper warming before taking foods avoid red meat avoid fat get screening especially those patients in whom there is a high risk or high risk individuals need to undergo endoscopy once in a year initially then every three yearly do not neglect the symptoms like dysphagia lack of appetite loss of weight vomiting
The surgeon completes the fourth layer of the jejunojejunal anastomosis, ensuring the connection between the jejunal segments is secure.
The surgeon performs a pharyngeal genostomy, preparing to support post-operative nutrition either through Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN) or by creating a feeding jejunostomy (FJ) for the patient.

The surgeon places a drain over the duodenal stump and the gastrojejunostomy (GG) closure, using loop rolling with nylon sutures approximately fifteen centimeters distal to the jejunal-jejunal anastomosis.
